QF.id: Evaluating Fund Performance and Fund Selection_10

question.text: Fund A has a Sharpe Ratio of 1.8 and Fund B has a Sharpe Ratio of 1.2. Both invest in large-cap equities. Which statement is correct?

answer.text: Fund B offers better risk-adjusted returns
Fund A offers better risk-adjusted returns
Both funds have identical risk profiles
Sharpe Ratio cannot be used to compare equity funds

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A higher Sharpe Ratio indicates better risk-adjusted returns — the fund generates more excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation). **The Trap:** Some candidates think a lower Sharpe Ratio is better (confusing it with expense ratio where lower is better) or believe Sharpe Ratio only applies to debt funds. **Cross-Exam Context:** Sharpe Ratio is one of the most commonly tested risk-adjusted measures. It is applicable across all asset classes and fund categories. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When presenting fund options to clients, include the Sharpe Ratio alongside raw returns. A fund with slightly lower returns but higher Sharpe may be more suitable for conservative investors.
